article_meta.title: The Corner NR PLUS Trade Breaking News: Americans Pay the Tariffs Their Government Imposes on Them By John R

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: The Corner NR PLUS Trade Breaking News: Americans Pay the Tariffs Their Government Imposes on Them By John R
SOURCE: Forgotten Fact Checks (National Review)

ARTICLE:

The Corner NR PLUS Trade Breaking News: Americans Pay the Tariffs Their Government Imposes on Them By John R. Puri January 20, 2026 5:40 PM Share on Facebook Share on Twitter Share on Flipboard Share on WhatsApp Email this article A shopper stands at a Target store ahead of the Thanksgiving holiday and traditional Black Friday sales in Chicago, Ill., November 21, 2023. (Vincent Alban/Reuters) Turns out Adam Smith got it right. Sign in here to read more. The Wall Street Journal reports on a new study from the Kiel Institute for the World Economy with a groundbreaking conclusion: Americans, not foreigners, are bearing almost the entire cost of U.S. tariffs, according to new research that contradicts a key claim by President Trump and suggests he might have a weaker hand in a reemerging trade war with Europe. Hold on a minute. President Trump has repeatedly claimed that tariffs — or taxes on imports that federal law requires importers to pay — are actually paid by foreign countries. Exporters are supposed to reduce their prices to maintain market share, so Americans essentially get free cash out of the deal, no? Apparently not: The German research echoes recent reports by the Budget Lab at Yale and economists at Harvard Business School, finding that only a small fraction of the tariff costs were being borne by foreign producers. By analyzing $4 trillion of shipments between January 2024 and November 2025, the Kiel Institute researchers found that foreign exporters absorbed only about 4% of the burden of last year's U.S. tariff increases by lowering their prices, while American consumers and importers absorbed 96%. This burden on Americans materializes in one of two ways. First, through increased prices for consumers, which the Kiel Institute expects to show up over time as companies' inventories run lower. That option coercively redirects money to the government that households would much rather spend or save for other purposes. The alternative, which may be even worse, is that Americans never get to purchase the imported products they want in the first place because tariffs make the cost of the transaction prohibitive. Again from the WSJ: The tariffs had a significant effect on trade volumes: Facing higher U.S. tariffs, Indian exporters maintained their prices but reduced the volume of shipments to the U.S. by 18%-24% relative to the European Union, Canada and Australia, the report found. Rather than acting as a tax on foreign producers, the tariffs functioned as a consumption tax on Americans, the report said. Either way, the purported free lunch of Trump's tariffs is a farce. Of the nearly $200 billion in federal revenue they raked in last year, almost all was paid by U.S. businesses and consumers. The president has completely ignored this data, of course, and is now threatening to punish Americans further by raising taxes on European imports until Denmark agrees to hand over Greenland. Can you identify the logical reasoning there? The fact that Europe is upset about Trump's threatened tariffs does not change who pays them. Both buyers and sellers benefit from voluntary economic transactions, so blocking them with tariffs hurts both parties. But while European firms lose revenue from exports, Americans lose both the money they already earned and their choices in how to spend it. Turns out, that Adam Smith fellow knew something about economics when he wrote that famous book, The Wealth of Nations, 250 years ago: The burden of trade barriers is paid, overwhelmingly, by the country that imposes them. return-icon Return to The Corner John R. Puri is the Thomas L. Rhodes Fellow at National Review. More in Trade How the Supreme Court May Untangle the Tariff Knot How the Supreme Court May Untangle the Tariff Knot The Tariffs Aren't Helping The Tariffs Aren't Helping Speaker Johnson Proves the Necessity of Reviving the Nondelegation Doctrine Speaker Johnson Proves the Necessity of Reviving the Nondelegation Doctrine Major Questions About the Tariff Argument Major Questions About the Tariff Argument
---

{
  "summary": "A recent study by the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, reported by The Wall Street Journal and analyzed in National Review, challenges the Trump administration’s claim that U.S.-imposed tariffs are paid by foreign producers. Instead, the data shows that American consumers and importers are absorbing nearly the entire cost of these tariffs. Analyzing $4 trillion in trade between January 2024 and November 2025, researchers found only about 4% of the tariff burden was shifted to foreign exporters, while 96% was borne by Americans. This cost materializes as either higher prices for consumers as inventories run down or through reduced consumer choice, as certain imports become prohibitively expensive. For example, Indian exporters maintained their prices against U.S. tariffs but shipped 18%-24% less to the U.S. compared to other countries. The article criticizes the administration’s narrative of tariffs as a tool for economic advantage, concluding instead that they act as a domestic consumption tax, reducing Americans’ purchasing power and options. The findings echo similar results from Yale and Harvard economists, reinforcing the classical economic view that trade barriers primarily harm the country imposing them.",
  "key_facts": [
    "A Kiel Institute study found American consumers and importers pay 96% of recent U.S. tariffs, with only 4% borne by foreign exporters.",
    "The findings contradict former President Trump's claim that tariffs are paid by foreign countries.",
    "Tariffs result in higher prices for U.S. consumers and reduced access to imported goods, functioning as a domestic consumption tax.",
    "Indian exporters did not lower their prices in response to tariffs but reduced shipments to the U.S. by 18%-24%.",
    "The research supports economic theories by Adam Smith and is echoed by studies from Yale and Harvard, indicating trade barriers mainly harm the imposing nation."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "National Review",
    "Wall Street Journal",
    "Kiel Institute for the World Economy",
    "President Trump's administration",
    "Academic economists (Yale, Harvard)"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: A recent study by the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, reported by The Wall Street Journal and analyzed in National Review, challenges the Trump administration’s claim that U.S.-imposed tariffs are paid by foreign producers. Instead, the data shows that American consumers and importers are absorbing nearly the entire cost of these tariffs. Analyzing $4 trillion in trade between January 2024 and November 2025, researchers found only about 4% of the tariff burden was shifted to foreign exporters, while 96% was borne by Americans. This cost materializes as either higher prices for consumers as inventories run down or through reduced consumer choice, as certain imports become prohibitively expensive. For example, Indian exporters maintained their prices against U.S. tariffs but shipped 18%-24% less to the U.S. compared to other countries. The article criticizes the administration’s narrative of tariffs as a tool for economic advantage, concluding instead that they act as a domestic consumption tax, reducing Americans’ purchasing power and options. The findings echo similar results from Yale and Harvard economists, reinforcing the classical economic view that trade barriers primarily harm the country imposing them.
key_facts: A Kiel Institute study found American consumers and importers pay 96% of recent U.S. tariffs, with only 4% borne by foreign exporters.
The findings contradict former President Trump's claim that tariffs are paid by foreign countries.
Tariffs result in higher prices for U.S. consumers and reduced access to imported goods, functioning as a domestic consumption tax.
Indian exporters did not lower their prices in response to tariffs but reduced shipments to the U.S. by 18%-24%.
The research supports economic theories by Adam Smith and is echoed by studies from Yale and Harvard, indicating trade barriers mainly harm the imposing nation.
sources_represented: Kiel Institute for the World Economy
